params.text:
Why infection prevention and control matters

Infection prevention and control (IPC) is everyone's business. The people you support in home care and residential settings are often older, frail, or living with long-term conditions, which means their bodies fight off infection less easily. A germ that gives you a mild cold could put someone you care for in hospital.

Good IPC is also a legal duty. The Health and Social Care Act 2008 Code of Practice on the prevention and control of infections sets out what registered providers must do, and the Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects against it. Guidance from UKHSA, NICE (including quality standard QS61) and the NHS shapes safe day-to-day practice.

The good news: most infections are preventable. Simple, consistent actions - especially cleaning your hands at the right moments - break the routes germs use to spread. This module gives you the practical knowledge to protect the people you support, your colleagues, your household and yourself.

  • Understand how infection spreads and how to stop it.
  • Apply standard precautions on every visit, for every person.
  • Know when to escalate - a spreading outbreak, or a possible case of sepsis.

params.question: Standard infection control precautions should be applied to everyone you care for, regardless of whether they have a known infection.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Because a person can carry an infection without any obvious signs, standard precautions must be applied to everyone, on every occasion.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. You cannot always tell who is carrying an infection, so standard precautions are used for every person, every time.
